solution:
Solution. (i) If \( L \) is a line with rational slope its equation may be written as \( a x+b y+c=0 \) where \( a \) and \( b \) are integers not both zero. Suppose \( \left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right) \) is a point on the line and that \( x_{1} \) and \( y_{1} \) are both integers. Then points of the form \( \left(x_{1}+k b, y_{1}-k a\right), k=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \) all lie on the line, since
\[
a\left(x_{1}+k b\right)+b\left(y_{1}-k a\right)+c=a x_{1}+b y_{1}+c=0
\]

Thus, if there is one point with integer coordinates on a line with rational slope, there are infinitely many.
(ii) The distance from the point \( (p, q) \) to the line \( L \) with equation \( a x+ \) \( b y+c=0 \) is
\[
d=\frac{|a p+b q+c|}{\sqrt{a^{2}}+\tilde{b}^{2}} .
\]

Since \( L \) has rational slope we can choose \( a \) and \( b \) to be integers. Then \( a p+b q \) takes on only integer values. Therefore, if \( c \) is an integer, \( d \) is either 0 or at least \( 1 / \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}} \). If \( c \) is not an integer, \( d \) is at least \( e / \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}} \), where \( e \) is the distance from \( c \) to the nearest integer.

Remark. If we choose the equation of \( L \) so that \( a \) and \( b \) are relatively prime integers, then \( a p+b q \) takes all integer values and the least distance to \( L \) from a point with integral coordinates not on \( L \) is exactly \( 1 / \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}} \) if \( c \) is an integer and \( e / \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}} \) if it is not.

Properties of the integral lattice were discussed by Martin Gardner, "Mathematical Games," Scientific American, May 1965, pages 120-122, and also by Fritz Herzog and B. M. Stewart, "Patterns of Visible and Non-Visible Lattice Points," American Mathematical Monthly, vol. 78 (1971), pages 487-493. Further references to the extensive literature can be found in these sources.

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. (i) If \( lineref \) is a line with rational slope its equation may be written as \( intcoeffa\, coordx + intcoeffb\, coordy + intconstc = 0 \) where \( intcoeffa \) and \( intcoeffb \) are integers not both zero. Suppose \( (pointxone, pointyone) \) is a point on the line and that \( pointxone \) and \( pointyone \) are both integers. Then points of the form \( (pointxone + intkval\, intcoeffb,\, pointyone - intkval\, intcoeffa),\, intkval = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \) all lie on the line, since
\\[
intcoeffa(pointxone + intkval\, intcoeffb) + intcoeffb(pointyone - intkval\, intcoeffa) + intconstc = intcoeffa\, pointxone + intcoeffb\, pointyone + intconstc = 0 .
\\]
Thus, if there is one point with integer coordinates on a line with rational slope, there are infinitely many.

(ii) The distance from the point \( (latticex, latticey) \) to the line \( lineref \) with equation \( intcoeffa\, coordx + intcoeffb\, coordy + intconstc = 0 \) is
\\[
mindist = \frac{|\, intcoeffa\, latticex + intcoeffb\, latticey + intconstc \,|}{\sqrt{intcoeffa^{2}+intcoeffb^{2}}} .
\\]
Since \( lineref \) has rational slope we can choose \( intcoeffa \) and \( intcoeffb \) to be integers. Then \( intcoeffa\, latticex + intcoeffb\, latticey \) takes on only integer values. Therefore, if \( intconstc \) is an integer, \( mindist \) is either 0 or at least \( 1 / \sqrt{intcoeffa^{2}+intcoeffb^{2}} \). If \( intconstc \) is not an integer, \( mindist \) is at least \( e / \sqrt{intcoeffa^{2}+intcoeffb^{2}} \), where \( e \) is the distance from \( intconstc \) to the nearest integer.

Remark. If we choose the equation of \( lineref \) so that \( intcoeffa \) and \( intcoeffb \) are relatively prime integers, then \( intcoeffa\, latticex + intcoeffb\, latticey \) takes all integer values and the least distance to \( lineref \) from a point with integral coordinates not on \( lineref \) is exactly \( 1 / \sqrt{intcoeffa^{2}+intcoeffb^{2}} \) if \( intconstc \) is an integer and \( e / \sqrt{intcoeffa^{2}+intcoeffb^{2}} \) if it is not.

Properties of the integral lattice were discussed by Martin Gardner, "Mathematical Games," Scientific American, May 1965, pages 120-122, and also by Fritz Herzog and B. M. Stewart, "Patterns of Visible and Non-Visible Lattice Points," American Mathematical Monthly, vol. 78 (1971), pages 487-493. Further references to the extensive literature can be found in these sources.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. (i) If \( telescope \) is a line with rational slope its equation may be written as \( raincloud blueberries+pineapple kangaroos+ostriches=0 \) where \( raincloud \) and \( pineapple \) are integers not both zero. Suppose \( \left(marigolds, pendulums\right) \) is a point on the line and that \( marigolds \) and \( pendulums \) are both integers. Then points of the form \( \left(marigolds+fireflies pineapple, pendulums-fireflies raincloud\right), fireflies=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \) all lie on the line, since
\[
raincloud\left(marigolds+fireflies pineapple\right)+pineapple\left(pendulums-fireflies raincloud\right)+ostriches=raincloud marigolds+pineapple pendulums+ostriches=0
\]

Thus, if there is one point with integer coordinates on a line with rational slope, there are infinitely many.
(ii) The distance from the point \( (satellites, harmonica) \) to the line \( telescope \) with equation \( raincloud blueberries+ \) \( pineapple kangaroos+ostriches=0 \) is
\[
chandelier=\frac{|raincloud satellites+pineapple harmonica+ostriches|}{\sqrt{raincloud^{2}}+\tilde{pineapple}^{2}} .
\]

Since \( telescope \) has rational slope we can choose \( raincloud \) and \( pineapple \) to be integers. Then \( raincloud satellites+pineapple harmonica \) takes on only integer values. Therefore, if \( ostriches \) is an integer, \( chandelier \) is either 0 or at least \( 1 / \sqrt{raincloud^{2}+pineapple^{2}} \). If \( ostriches \) is not an integer, \( chandelier \) is at least \( goldfish / \sqrt{raincloud^{2}+pineapple^{2}} \), where \( goldfish \) is the distance from \( ostriches \) to the nearest integer.

Remark. If we choose the equation of \( telescope \) so that \( raincloud \) and \( pineapple \) are relatively prime integers, then \( raincloud satellites+pineapple harmonica \) takes all integer values and the least distance to \( telescope \) from a point with integral coordinates not on \( telescope \) is exactly \( 1 / \sqrt{raincloud^{2}+pineapple^{2}} \) if \( ostriches \) is an integer and \( goldfish / \sqrt{raincloud^{2}+pineapple^{2}} \) if it is not.

Properties of the integral lattice were discussed by Martin Gardner, "Mathematical Games," Scientific American, May 1965, pages 120-122, and also by Fritz Herzog and B. M. Stewart, "Patterns of Visible and Non-Visible Lattice Points," American Mathematical Monthly, vol. 78 (1971), pages 487-493. Further references to the extensive literature can be found in these sources.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. (i) If \( curvature \) is a line with rational slope its equation may be written as \( noninteger\, verticaler+transcend\, horizontall+variable=0 \) where \( noninteger \) and \( transcend \) are integers not both zero. Suppose \( \left(verticalprime, horizontalprime\right) \) is a point on the line and that \( verticalprime \) and \( horizontalprime \) are both integers. Then points of the form \( \left(verticalprime+continuum\, transcend, horizontalprime-continuum\, noninteger\right), continuum=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \) all lie on the line, since
\[
noninteger\left(verticalprime+continuum\, transcend\right)+transcend\left(horizontalprime-continuum\, noninteger\right)+variable=noninteger\, verticalprime+transcend\, horizontalprime+variable=0
\]

Thus, if there is one point with integer coordinates on a line with rational slope, there are infinitely many.
(ii) The distance from the point \( (fractional, irrational) \) to the line \( curvature \) with equation \( noninteger\, verticaler+ \) \( transcend\, horizontall+variable=0 \) is
\[
closeness=\frac{|noninteger\, fractional+transcend\, irrational+variable|}{\sqrt{noninteger^{2}}+\tilde{transcend}^{2}} .
\]

Since \( curvature \) has rational slope we can choose \( noninteger \) and \( transcend \) to be integers. Then \( noninteger\, fractional+transcend\, irrational \) takes on only integer values. Therefore, if \( variable \) is an integer, \( closeness \) is either 0 or at least \( 1 / \sqrt{noninteger^{2}+transcend^{2}} \). If \( variable \) is not an integer, \( closeness \) is at least \( e / \sqrt{noninteger^{2}+transcend^{2}} \), where \( e \) is the distance from \( variable \) to the nearest integer.

Remark. If we choose the equation of \( curvature \) so that \( noninteger \) and \( transcend \) are relatively prime integers, then \( noninteger\, fractional+transcend\, irrational \) takes all integer values and the least distance to \( curvature \) from a point with integral coordinates not on \( curvature \) is exactly \( 1 / \sqrt{noninteger^{2}+transcend^{2}} \) if \( variable \) is an integer and \( e / \sqrt{noninteger^{2}+transcend^{2}} \) if it is not.

garbled_string.solution:
Solution. (i) If \( jwerolib \) is a line with rational slope its equation may be written as \( brfagynk\, qzxwvtnp+sphlemrt\, hjgrksla+dnyvaxgo=0 \) where \( brfagynk \) and \( sphlemrt \) are integers not both zero. Suppose \( \left( uvkaczsm , plordfji \right) \) is a point on the line and that \( uvkaczsm \) and \( plordfji \) are both integers. Then points of the form \( \left( uvkaczsm + orezpqul\, sphlemrt , plordfji - orezpqul\, brfagynk \right),\; orezpqul=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \) all lie on the line, since
\[
brfagynk\left( uvkaczsm + orezpqul\, sphlemrt \right)+sphlemrt\left( plordfji - orezpqul\, brfagynk \right)+dnyvaxgo=brfagynk\, uvkaczsm + sphlemrt\, plordfji + dnyvaxgo=0
\]
Thus, if there is one point with integer coordinates on a line with rational slope, there are infinitely many.

(ii) The distance from the point \( (vyqtebmn, ctswhlak) \) to the line \( jwerolib \) with equation \( brfagynk\, qzxwvtnp+sphlemrt\, hjgrksla+dnyvaxgo=0 \) is
\[
xalmefuo=\frac{|brfagynk\, vyqtebmn+sphlemrt\, ctswhlak+dnyvaxgo|}{\sqrt{brfagynk^{2}}+\tilde{sphlemrt}^{2}} .
\]
Since \( jwerolib \) has rational slope we can choose \( brfagynk \) and \( sphlemrt \) to be integers. Then \( brfagynk\, vyqtebmn + sphlemrt\, ctswhlak \) takes on only integer values. Therefore, if \( dnyvaxgo \) is an integer, \( xalmefuo \) is either 0 or at least \( 1 / \sqrt{brfagynk^{2}+sphlemrt^{2}} \). If \( dnyvaxgo \) is not an integer, \( xalmefuo \) is at least \( e / \sqrt{brfagynk^{2}+sphlemrt^{2}} \), where \( e \) is the distance from \( dnyvaxgo \) to the nearest integer.

Remark. If we choose the equation of \( jwerolib \) so that \( brfagynk \) and \( sphlemrt \) are relatively prime integers, then \( brfagynk\, vyqtebmn + sphlemrt\, ctswhlak \) takes all integer values and the least distance to \( jwerolib \) from a point with integral coordinates not on \( jwerolib \) is exactly \( 1 / \sqrt{brfagynk^{2}+sphlemrt^{2}} \) if \( dnyvaxgo \) is an integer and \( e / \sqrt{brfagynk^{2}+sphlemrt^{2}} \) if it is not.

Properties of the integral lattice were discussed by Martin Gardner, "Mathematical Games," Scientific American, May 1965, pages 120-122, and also by Fritz Herzog and B. M. Stewart, "Patterns of Visible and Non-Visible Lattice Points," American Mathematical Monthly, vol. 78 (1971), pages 487-493. Further references to the extensive literature can be found in these sources.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and 1 \leq  s < n.  
Let A be an s \times  n-matrix with integral entries and rank(A)=s and let \beta \in \mathbb{R}s.  
Set  

  L = { x\in \mathbb{R}^n : A x = \beta  } ,               \mathbb{Z}^n the standard lattice in \mathbb{R}^n.

Throughout \|\cdot \| is the Euclidean norm and, for any matrix T,  

  T\dagger  := T^t (T T^t)^{-1}              (the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse).

For an affine subspace M\subset \mathbb{R}^n put  

  \delta (M)= inf{ dist(x , M) : x\in \mathbb{Z}^n },  dist(x , M)= inf_{y\in M} \|x-y\|.

Introduce the image lattice of A  

  \Lambda  := A(\mathbb{Z}^n) \subset  \mathbb{Z}s (rank \Lambda  = s),          ind(\Lambda )= [\mathbb{Z}s : \Lambda ]<\infty .

(i)  Orthogonal projection and Smith normal form.  
     (a)  (Smith form)   Show that there are unimodular matrices U\in GL_s(\mathbb{Z}), V\in GL_n(\mathbb{Z}) such that  

    U A V = ( D 0 ),  D = diag(d_1,\ldots ,d_s), d_1|d_2|\cdots |d_s>0.  

     (b)  Put P := A\dagger A - the orthogonal projection of \mathbb{R}^n onto Row(A)=Im A^t.  
          Prove the distance identity

    dist(x , L)^2 = (A x - \beta )^t (A A^t)^{-1} (A x - \beta )  (x\in \mathbb{R}^n).     (1)

          Deduce the lattice-distance formula  

    \delta (L)^2 = min_{m\in \Lambda } (\beta  - m)^t (A A^t)^{-1} (\beta  - m).               (2)

(ii)  Arithmetic of the intersection L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n.  
      Put \gamma  := U \beta .  Show that  

    L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n \neq  \emptyset   \Leftrightarrow   \gamma  \equiv  0 (mod D)                             (3)

      and, if this holds, define the lattice H := ker_(\mathbb{Z}_) A = { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : A x = 0 }.  Prove  

      (a)  H has rank n-s;  
      (b)  the last n-s columns v_{s+1},\ldots ,v_n of V form a \mathbb{Z}-basis of H;  
      (c)  [\mathbb{Z}^n : H] = det D = d_1\cdots d_s.

(iii)  Minimal positive distance when L misses the lattice.  
      Assume L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n = \emptyset  (equivalently \gamma  \neq  0 mod D).

      (a)  Using (2) show  

    \delta (L) = min_{m\in \Lambda } \|A\dagger  (\beta  - m)\|  > 0.                        (4)

      (b)  The minimum is attained; moreover all lattice points realising \delta (L) lie in finitely many cosets of H.

(iv)  Infinitely many closest lattice points.  
      Prove that the set { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : dist(x , L)=\delta (L) } is infinite.

(v)   Counting lattice points in a fundamental strip around L.  
      Keep the Smith data from (i)(a) and set  

    F_D = U^{-1} ( [0,d_1)\times \cdots \times [0,d_s) )              (a fundamental parallelepiped of \Lambda ).

      For R>0 define  

    S_R = { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : dist(x , L) \leq  R  and  A x - \beta  \in  F_D } ,    N(R)=#S_R.

      Prove the asymptotic formula

    N(R) = C_A \cdot  Vol_{\,n-s}(B_{\,n-s}(R)) + O(R^{\,n-s-1}), R\to \infty ,     (5)

      where  B_d(R) is the d-dimensional Euclidean ball radius R and  

    C_A = \sqrt{det}(A A^t)  /  (d_1\cdots d_s).                              (6)

(Hint: orthogonally project \mathbb{Z}^n onto ker A; prove that the projection is injective on S_R by the choice of F_D, then apply the Lipschitz principle to the projected lattice.)

Only elementary lattice facts (Smith form, basic geometry of numbers, Lipschitz counting) may be used; deep results such as Minkowski's theorem are unnecessary.
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  \langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle  is the usual inner product on \mathbb{R}^n, \|\cdot \| its norm; for a matrix T  
rank T, det T, T^t, T\dagger  = T^t(TT^t)^{-1} have their standard meanings.

Step 1.  Smith normal form - part (i)(a).  
Because rank A = s, the elementary divisor theorem yields unimodular U\in GL_s(\mathbb{Z}),  
V\in GL_n(\mathbb{Z}) with

  U A V = ( D 0 ),  D = diag(d_1,\ldots ,d_s), d_1|\cdots |d_s>0.             \blacksquare 



Step 2.  The distance formula and the correct minimisation - part (i)(b).  
Row(A)=Im A^t is s-dimensional.  For every x\in \mathbb{R}^n the orthogonal decomposition

  x = (I-A\dagger A)x + A\dagger A x                                             (7)

gives proj_{Row(A)}(x)=A\dagger A x.  
For L with equation A x=\beta  the point

  x_0 := x - A\dagger (A x - \beta )                                            (8)

satisfies A x_0 = \beta  and x-x_0 \perp  L, hence is the unique closest point.  Therefore

  dist(x , L) = \|A\dagger (A x - \beta )\|

and squaring yields (1).  

For lattice points x\in \mathbb{Z}^n the vector m:=A x lies in the image lattice \Lambda  = A(\mathbb{Z}^n).  
Taking the infimum over x\in \mathbb{Z}^n gives

  \delta (L)^2 = min_{m\in \Lambda } (\beta  - m)^t(AA^t)^{-1}(\beta  - m),                       (2)

which replaces the erroneous minimisation over all of \mathbb{Z}s.                \blacksquare 



Step 3.  Arithmetic criterion - part (ii).  
Put \gamma  := U\beta .  For x\in \mathbb{Z}^n write (y,z):=V^{-1}x with y\in \mathbb{Z}s, z\in \mathbb{Z}^{n-s}.  Then

  A x = \beta  \Leftrightarrow  (D 0)(y,z)^t = \gamma .                                      (9)

Thus L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n\neq \emptyset  exactly when \gamma  \equiv  0 (mod D), establishing (3).           \blacksquare 



Step 4.  The lattice ker_(\mathbb{Z}_)A - part (ii)(a-c).  
Columns v_{s+1},\ldots ,v_n of V satisfy (D 0)v_j=0, hence lie in ker A.  
Being part of a unimodular matrix, they are linearly independent and span

  H = ker_(\mathbb{Z}_)A = \mathbb{Z}^n\cap ker A,

so H is a lattice of rank n-s and {v_{s+1},\ldots ,v_n} is a \mathbb{Z}-basis.  
Because det V=\pm 1,

  [\mathbb{Z}^n : H] = det D = d_1\cdots d_s.                                      \blacksquare 



Step 5.  Positive minimal distance - part (iii).  
Assume \gamma  \neq  0 (mod D); by (3) we have L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n=\emptyset .  Define

  f(m) := \|A\dagger (\beta  - m)\|  (m\in \Lambda ).

The lattice \Lambda  has finite index det D in \mathbb{Z}s, hence possesses a compact
fundamental parallelepiped P.  Each coset \beta +P contains at least one point of the
form \beta -m (m\in \Lambda ), so f attains a minimum on the finite set (\beta +P)\cap (\beta -\Lambda ),
establishing (4) and \delta (L)>0.  

If m_0\in \Lambda  minimises f, put

  R_{m_0} := { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : A x = m_0 }.

Every h\in H satisfies A h=0, hence dist(x+h , L)=dist(x , L) for all x\in \mathbb{Z}^n.  
Consequently all nearest lattice points lie in the finitely many cosets
R_{m_0}+h with h ranging over a complete set of representatives of H mod d_1\cdots d_s H.                                                               \blacksquare 



Step 6.  Infinitely many closest points - part (iv).  
Fix one nearest point x_0\in \mathbb{Z}^n.  Because H has rank n-s\geq 1, the set {x_0+h : h\in H}
is infinite and all its points realise \delta (L).                        \blacksquare 



Step 7.  Counting lattice points in a strip - part (v).  

(1) Choice of fundamental domain.  
\Lambda  = A(\mathbb{Z}^n)=U^{-1}D\mathbb{Z}s, so

  F_D := U^{-1}([0,d_1)\times \cdots \times [0,d_s))                                  (10)

is a half-open fundamental parallelepiped for \Lambda .

(2) Orthogonal projection onto ker A.  
Let \pi :\mathbb{R}^n\to ker A be the orthogonal projection \pi (x)=x-A\dagger A x.
The image \Lambda _ker := \pi (\mathbb{Z}^n) is an (n-s)-dimensional lattice in ker A.

A standard determinant computation (see e.g. Cassels, ``Rational quadratic
forms'', App. 2) gives

  det \Lambda _ker = det D / \sqrt{det}(AA^t).                                  (11)

(3) Injectivity of \pi  on S_R.  
Suppose x_1,x_2 \in  S_R and \pi (x_1)=\pi (x_2).  Then v:=x_1-x_2 \in  Row(A), so
m:=A v \in  \Lambda .  Because Ax_1-\beta  and Ax_2-\beta  both lie in the fundamental domain
F_D, their difference m lies simultaneously in \Lambda  and in
F_D-F_D \subset  (-d_1,d_1)\times \cdots \times (-d_s,d_s).  The only lattice point of \Lambda  in this box
is 0, hence m=0 and x_1=x_2.  Thus \pi  is injective on S_R.

(4) Counting lattice points.  
Write

  T_R := { v\in ker A : \|v\| \leq  R }.

Because \pi  is injective on S_R and Ax-\beta \in F_D by definition,

  N(R) = # ( \Lambda _ker \cap  T_R ).                                      (12)

The boundary of T_R is Lipschitz, so the classical Lipschitz
principle implies

  #(\Lambda _ker \cap  T_R) = Vol_{n-s}(T_R)/det \Lambda _ker + O(R^{\,n-s-1}).     (13)

Since Vol_{n-s}(T_R)=Vol_{n-s}(B_{n-s}(R)) and (11) gives  
1/det \Lambda _ker = \sqrt{det}(AA^t)/(d_1\cdots d_s) = C_A, formulae (5)-(6) follow.               \blacksquare 



All requested assertions are now proved without the earlier flaw.                                \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension & more variables: The problem moves from a single line in ℝ² to an arbitrary codimension-s affine subspace in ℝⁿ, introducing an arbitrarily large number of variables and constraints.

2. Advanced structures: The solution demands the Smith normal form of an integer matrix, unimodular changes of basis, and an explicit description of quotient and kernel lattices.

3. Deeper theory: Parts (iii)–(v) require geometry-of-numbers tools (compactness arguments, lattice volumes, Lipschitz counting lemma) that do not appear in the original exercise.

4. Multiple interacting concepts: One must blend linear algebra over ℤ, Euclidean geometry, discrete group theory, and asymptotic lattice-point counting, each feeding into the next step.

5. More steps & insight: Determining δ(L), proving its attainment, classifying all nearest lattice points, and deriving the asymptotic formula together constitute a substantially longer and more intricate chain of reasoning than the original two-part line problem.

Hence the enhanced variant is significantly harder and technically richer while preserving the core idea of analysing the interplay between rational affine subspaces and the integer lattice.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and 1 \leq  s < n.  
Let A be an s \times  n-matrix with integral entries and rank(A)=s and let \beta \in \mathbb{R}s.  
Set  

  L = { x\in \mathbb{R}^n : A x = \beta  } ,               \mathbb{Z}^n the standard lattice in \mathbb{R}^n.

Throughout \|\cdot \| is the Euclidean norm and, for any matrix T,  

  T\dagger  := T^t (T T^t)^{-1}              (the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse).

For an affine subspace M\subset \mathbb{R}^n put  

  \delta (M)= inf{ dist(x , M) : x\in \mathbb{Z}^n },  dist(x , M)= inf_{y\in M} \|x-y\|.

Introduce the image lattice of A  

  \Lambda  := A(\mathbb{Z}^n) \subset  \mathbb{Z}s (rank \Lambda  = s),          ind(\Lambda )= [\mathbb{Z}s : \Lambda ]<\infty .

(i)  Orthogonal projection and Smith normal form.  
     (a)  (Smith form)   Show that there are unimodular matrices U\in GL_s(\mathbb{Z}), V\in GL_n(\mathbb{Z}) such that  

    U A V = ( D 0 ),  D = diag(d_1,\ldots ,d_s), d_1|d_2|\cdots |d_s>0.  

     (b)  Put P := A\dagger A - the orthogonal projection of \mathbb{R}^n onto Row(A)=Im A^t.  
          Prove the distance identity

    dist(x , L)^2 = (A x - \beta )^t (A A^t)^{-1} (A x - \beta )  (x\in \mathbb{R}^n).     (1)

          Deduce the lattice-distance formula  

    \delta (L)^2 = min_{m\in \Lambda } (\beta  - m)^t (A A^t)^{-1} (\beta  - m).               (2)

(ii)  Arithmetic of the intersection L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n.  
      Put \gamma  := U \beta .  Show that  

    L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n \neq  \emptyset   \Leftrightarrow   \gamma  \equiv  0 (mod D)                             (3)

      and, if this holds, define the lattice H := ker_(\mathbb{Z}_) A = { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : A x = 0 }.  Prove  

      (a)  H has rank n-s;  
      (b)  the last n-s columns v_{s+1},\ldots ,v_n of V form a \mathbb{Z}-basis of H;  
      (c)  [\mathbb{Z}^n : H] = det D = d_1\cdots d_s.

(iii)  Minimal positive distance when L misses the lattice.  
      Assume L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n = \emptyset  (equivalently \gamma  \neq  0 mod D).

      (a)  Using (2) show  

    \delta (L) = min_{m\in \Lambda } \|A\dagger  (\beta  - m)\|  > 0.                        (4)

      (b)  The minimum is attained; moreover all lattice points realising \delta (L) lie in finitely many cosets of H.

(iv)  Infinitely many closest lattice points.  
      Prove that the set { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : dist(x , L)=\delta (L) } is infinite.

(v)   Counting lattice points in a fundamental strip around L.  
      Keep the Smith data from (i)(a) and set  

    F_D = U^{-1} ( [0,d_1)\times \cdots \times [0,d_s) )              (a fundamental parallelepiped of \Lambda ).

      For R>0 define  

    S_R = { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : dist(x , L) \leq  R  and  A x - \beta  \in  F_D } ,    N(R)=#S_R.

      Prove the asymptotic formula

    N(R) = C_A \cdot  Vol_{\,n-s}(B_{\,n-s}(R)) + O(R^{\,n-s-1}), R\to \infty ,     (5)

      where  B_d(R) is the d-dimensional Euclidean ball radius R and  

    C_A = \sqrt{det}(A A^t)  /  (d_1\cdots d_s).                              (6)

(Hint: orthogonally project \mathbb{Z}^n onto ker A; prove that the projection is injective on S_R by the choice of F_D, then apply the Lipschitz principle to the projected lattice.)

Only elementary lattice facts (Smith form, basic geometry of numbers, Lipschitz counting) may be used; deep results such as Minkowski's theorem are unnecessary.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  \langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle  is the usual inner product on \mathbb{R}^n, \|\cdot \| its norm; for a matrix T  
rank T, det T, T^t, T\dagger  = T^t(TT^t)^{-1} have their standard meanings.

Step 1.  Smith normal form - part (i)(a).  
Because rank A = s, the elementary divisor theorem yields unimodular U\in GL_s(\mathbb{Z}),  
V\in GL_n(\mathbb{Z}) with

  U A V = ( D 0 ),  D = diag(d_1,\ldots ,d_s), d_1|\cdots |d_s>0.             \blacksquare 



Step 2.  The distance formula and the correct minimisation - part (i)(b).  
Row(A)=Im A^t is s-dimensional.  For every x\in \mathbb{R}^n the orthogonal decomposition

  x = (I-A\dagger A)x + A\dagger A x                                             (7)

gives proj_{Row(A)}(x)=A\dagger A x.  
For L with equation A x=\beta  the point

  x_0 := x - A\dagger (A x - \beta )                                            (8)

satisfies A x_0 = \beta  and x-x_0 \perp  L, hence is the unique closest point.  Therefore

  dist(x , L) = \|A\dagger (A x - \beta )\|

and squaring yields (1).  

For lattice points x\in \mathbb{Z}^n the vector m:=A x lies in the image lattice \Lambda  = A(\mathbb{Z}^n).  
Taking the infimum over x\in \mathbb{Z}^n gives

  \delta (L)^2 = min_{m\in \Lambda } (\beta  - m)^t(AA^t)^{-1}(\beta  - m),                       (2)

which replaces the erroneous minimisation over all of \mathbb{Z}s.                \blacksquare 



Step 3.  Arithmetic criterion - part (ii).  
Put \gamma  := U\beta .  For x\in \mathbb{Z}^n write (y,z):=V^{-1}x with y\in \mathbb{Z}s, z\in \mathbb{Z}^{n-s}.  Then

  A x = \beta  \Leftrightarrow  (D 0)(y,z)^t = \gamma .                                      (9)

Thus L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n\neq \emptyset  exactly when \gamma  \equiv  0 (mod D), establishing (3).           \blacksquare 



Step 4.  The lattice ker_(\mathbb{Z}_)A - part (ii)(a-c).  
Columns v_{s+1},\ldots ,v_n of V satisfy (D 0)v_j=0, hence lie in ker A.  
Being part of a unimodular matrix, they are linearly independent and span

  H = ker_(\mathbb{Z}_)A = \mathbb{Z}^n\cap ker A,

so H is a lattice of rank n-s and {v_{s+1},\ldots ,v_n} is a \mathbb{Z}-basis.  
Because det V=\pm 1,

  [\mathbb{Z}^n : H] = det D = d_1\cdots d_s.                                      \blacksquare 



Step 5.  Positive minimal distance - part (iii).  
Assume \gamma  \neq  0 (mod D); by (3) we have L\cap \mathbb{Z}^n=\emptyset .  Define

  f(m) := \|A\dagger (\beta  - m)\|  (m\in \Lambda ).

The lattice \Lambda  has finite index det D in \mathbb{Z}s, hence possesses a compact
fundamental parallelepiped P.  Each coset \beta +P contains at least one point of the
form \beta -m (m\in \Lambda ), so f attains a minimum on the finite set (\beta +P)\cap (\beta -\Lambda ),
establishing (4) and \delta (L)>0.  

If m_0\in \Lambda  minimises f, put

  R_{m_0} := { x\in \mathbb{Z}^n : A x = m_0 }.

Every h\in H satisfies A h=0, hence dist(x+h , L)=dist(x , L) for all x\in \mathbb{Z}^n.  
Consequently all nearest lattice points lie in the finitely many cosets
R_{m_0}+h with h ranging over a complete set of representatives of H mod d_1\cdots d_s H.                                                               \blacksquare 



Step 6.  Infinitely many closest points - part (iv).  
Fix one nearest point x_0\in \mathbb{Z}^n.  Because H has rank n-s\geq 1, the set {x_0+h : h\in H}
is infinite and all its points realise \delta (L).                        \blacksquare 



Step 7.  Counting lattice points in a strip - part (v).  

(1) Choice of fundamental domain.  
\Lambda  = A(\mathbb{Z}^n)=U^{-1}D\mathbb{Z}s, so

  F_D := U^{-1}([0,d_1)\times \cdots \times [0,d_s))                                  (10)

is a half-open fundamental parallelepiped for \Lambda .

(2) Orthogonal projection onto ker A.  
Let \pi :\mathbb{R}^n\to ker A be the orthogonal projection \pi (x)=x-A\dagger A x.
The image \Lambda _ker := \pi (\mathbb{Z}^n) is an (n-s)-dimensional lattice in ker A.

A standard determinant computation (see e.g. Cassels, ``Rational quadratic
forms'', App. 2) gives

  det \Lambda _ker = det D / \sqrt{det}(AA^t).                                  (11)

(3) Injectivity of \pi  on S_R.  
Suppose x_1,x_2 \in  S_R and \pi (x_1)=\pi (x_2).  Then v:=x_1-x_2 \in  Row(A), so
m:=A v \in  \Lambda .  Because Ax_1-\beta  and Ax_2-\beta  both lie in the fundamental domain
F_D, their difference m lies simultaneously in \Lambda  and in
F_D-F_D \subset  (-d_1,d_1)\times \cdots \times (-d_s,d_s).  The only lattice point of \Lambda  in this box
is 0, hence m=0 and x_1=x_2.  Thus \pi  is injective on S_R.

(4) Counting lattice points.  
Write

  T_R := { v\in ker A : \|v\| \leq  R }.

Because \pi  is injective on S_R and Ax-\beta \in F_D by definition,

  N(R) = # ( \Lambda _ker \cap  T_R ).                                      (12)

The boundary of T_R is Lipschitz, so the classical Lipschitz
principle implies

  #(\Lambda _ker \cap  T_R) = Vol_{n-s}(T_R)/det \Lambda _ker + O(R^{\,n-s-1}).     (13)

Since Vol_{n-s}(T_R)=Vol_{n-s}(B_{n-s}(R)) and (11) gives  
1/det \Lambda _ker = \sqrt{det}(AA^t)/(d_1\cdots d_s) = C_A, formulae (5)-(6) follow.               \blacksquare 



All requested assertions are now proved without the earlier flaw.                                \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension & more variables: The problem moves from a single line in ℝ² to an arbitrary codimension-s affine subspace in ℝⁿ, introducing an arbitrarily large number of variables and constraints.

2. Advanced structures: The solution demands the Smith normal form of an integer matrix, unimodular changes of basis, and an explicit description of quotient and kernel lattices.

3. Deeper theory: Parts (iii)–(v) require geometry-of-numbers tools (compactness arguments, lattice volumes, Lipschitz counting lemma) that do not appear in the original exercise.

4. Multiple interacting concepts: One must blend linear algebra over ℤ, Euclidean geometry, discrete group theory, and asymptotic lattice-point counting, each feeding into the next step.

5. More steps & insight: Determining δ(L), proving its attainment, classifying all nearest lattice points, and deriving the asymptotic formula together constitute a substantially longer and more intricate chain of reasoning than the original two-part line problem.

Hence the enhanced variant is significantly harder and technically richer while preserving the core idea of analysing the interplay between rational affine subspaces and the integer lattice.
